schools.summary: Gill: Jarchi observes there is no mention of linen here, because these were not the priestly garments in which there was linen, but the cloths of service in which the holy things were wrapped; the witnesses note the making of cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron, of blue, purple, and scarlet, to do service in the holy place, as the LORD commanded Moses — the executing of the priestly vestments after the pattern (the garments commanded now made: 'of the blue, and purple, and scarlet, they made cloths of service... and made the holy garments for Aaron; as the LORD commanded Moses'; the cloths of service, that these, lacking linen, were not the linen priestly garments but the cloths in which the holy things were wrapped for carriage; the holy garments, 'the holy garments for Aaron,' the high priest's glorious vestments, now wrought; the rule, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' that all was done exactly as commanded, a refrain repeated through the chapter; the type before given, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ, our High Priest, and his people are clothed for God's service; that the making answered the command, the work done after the pattern).

themes: of the blue, and purple, and scarlet, they made cloths of service, to do service in the holy place, and made the holy garments for Aaron
as the LORD commanded Moses
the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron made of the rich colours, as the LORD commanded
the holy garments shadowing the righteousness and graces with which Christ our High Priest and his people are clothed

schools.summary: Gill: from here to the end of verse thirty-one is a very particular account of the making of the priest's garments, exactly according to the directions given to Moses; the witnesses note the making of the ephod of gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen — the executing of the ephod, the chief garment of the high priest, after the pattern (the ephod commanded now made: 'he made the ephod of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen'; that from this verse the account gives a very particular account of the making of the priest's garments, exactly according to the directions given to Moses; the ephod, 'the ephod of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen,' the chief and most glorious vestment, woven of the precious metal and the rich colours and linen; that the gold was wrought in, the colours and linen woven together with cunning work; the type before given, that the ephod, the high priest's chief garment, on which the names of the tribes were borne, shadows Christ, our High Priest, who bears his people before God; that the making answered the command exactly, the work done after the pattern; that the glory of the garment befitted the dignity of the office).

themes: And he made the ephod of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet
and fine twined linen
the ephod, the chief garment of the high priest, made exactly according to the directions given to Moses
the ephod, on which the tribes' names were borne, shadowing Christ our High Priest, who bears his people before God

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: they beat the gold into thin plates for the purpose, as supposed, of cutting it into wires or threads, to twist or twine being the common acceptation of the root; the witnesses note the gold beaten into thin plates and cut into wires, to be worked with cunning into the blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen — the curious working of the gold into the very fabric of the ephod (the gold wrought as thread into the garment: 'they did beat the gold into thin plates, and cut it into wires, to work it in the blue, and in the purple, and in the scarlet, and in the fine linen, with cunning work'; the working, 'beat the gold into thin plates, and cut it into wires,' that the gold was hammered thin and cut into fine wires or threads, to twist or twine; the use, 'to work it in the blue... purple... scarlet... fine linen, with cunning work,' that these golden threads were woven into the colours and linen, the gold running through the whole; the skill and labour, that this was a work of great art and care, the gold curiously interwoven; the meaning some draw, that the gold, glory, or faith more precious than gold, wrought through the whole garment may shadow the graces interwoven in the righteousness with which the saints are clothed; that the work of God's house called for the finest art and the costliest stuff).

themes: they did beat the gold into thin plates, and cut it into wires, to work it in the blue, and in the purple, and in the scarlet, and in the fine linen
with cunning work
the gold beaten thin and cut into wires, woven with cunning work into the ephod
some seeing the gold wrought through the whole shadow the graces interwoven in the righteousness with which the saints are clothed

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the onyx stones, possibly the Egyptian pebble, set in gold and graven with the names of the tribes, as before directed; the witnesses note the making of the onyx stones, enclosed in ouches of gold and engraved, like a signet, with the names of the children of Israel — the executing of the shoulder-stones bearing the tribes' names after the pattern (the names of Israel graven on the stones: 'they wrought onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold, graven, as signets are graven, with the names of the children of Israel'; the stones, 'onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold,' two precious stones set in gold settings, made as commanded; the engraving, 'graven, as signets are graven, with the names of the children of Israel,' that the names of the twelve tribes were cut into the stones, as a seal is cut, with deep and lasting workmanship; the great type before given, that these stones, with the tribes' names, set on the shoulders of the ephod, shadow Christ, our High Priest, who bears the names of his people graven, sure and lasting, and carries them on his shoulders, the place of strength; that the making answered the command exactly; that the names of God's people are dear to him and borne by their High Priest).

debates.question: What do the onyx stones, engraved with the tribes' names and set on the shoulders of the ephod, shadow?

sides.position: Christ, our High Priest, who bears the names of his people graven (sure and lasting, as a signet is cut) on his shoulders, the place of strength — the names of God's people dear to him and borne by their High Priest

themes: they wrought onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold, graven, as signets are graven
with the names of the children of Israel
the onyx stones set in gold and engraved, like a signet, with the names of the tribes
a type of Christ who bears the names of his people graven, sure and lasting, on his shoulders, the place of strength

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the stones for a memorial, as before directed (Exodus 28:12), that the high priest bore the names of the tribes before the LORD continually; the witnesses note the stones set on the shoulders of the ephod, to be a memorial of the children of Israel, as the LORD commanded Moses — the placing of the names on the shoulders, the high priest bearing his people in remembrance before God (the tribes borne for a memorial: 'he put them on the shoulders of the ephod, that they should be stones for a memorial to the children of Israel'; the placing, 'on the shoulders of the ephod,' that the two stones, with the tribes' names, were set on the two shoulder-pieces; the purpose, 'stones for a memorial to the children of Israel,' that the high priest, wearing them, bore the names of all the tribes before the LORD continually, for a memorial, that God might remember his people; the rule, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' the refrain of exact obedience; the great type before given, that Christ, our High Priest, bears his people on his shoulders, in his strength, presenting them and their names before God for a perpetual memorial; that we are remembered before God by our High Priest, borne on his strength; that the making answered the command).

themes: he put them on the shoulders of the ephod, that they should be stones for a memorial to the children of Israel
as the LORD commanded Moses
the stones with the tribes' names set on the shoulders of the ephod, a memorial before the LORD
a type of Christ who bears his people on his shoulders, in his strength, presenting them before God for a perpetual memorial

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the breastplate, made as before directed (Exodus 28:15), of cunning work, like the work of the ephod; the witnesses note the making of the breastplate, of cunning work like the ephod, of gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen — the executing of the breastplate, which bore the tribes' names on the heart, after the pattern (the breastplate commanded now made: 'he made the breastplate of cunning work, like the work of the ephod; of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen'; the work, 'of cunning work, like the work of the ephod,' the breastplate wrought with the same art and of the same precious materials as the ephod, made as commanded; that the breastplate was set with twelve stones, each graven with a tribe's name, and worn on the high priest's breast; the great type before given, that, as the shoulder-stones, the place of strength, bore the tribes, so the breastplate, the place of the heart, bore them, shadowing Christ, our High Priest, who bears his people not only on his shoulders, in his strength, but on his heart, in his love; that the making answered the command exactly; that we are borne both on the strength and on the love of our High Priest).

debates.question: What does the breastplate, bearing the tribes' names on the high priest's heart, shadow?

sides.position: Christ, our High Priest, who bears his people on his heart, in his love — as the shoulder-stones bore them on his shoulders, in his strength; we are borne both on the strength and on the love of our High Priest

themes: And he made the breastplate of cunning work, like the work of the ephod
of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen
the breastplate made like the ephod, worn on the high priest's breast, bearing the tribes on the heart
a type of Christ who bears his people on his heart, in his love, as on his shoulders, in his strength

schools.summary: Gill: the breastplate was made exactly according to the directions given to Moses, foursquare and doubled, a span in length and breadth; the witnesses note the making of the breastplate foursquare, doubled, a span in length and breadth — the executing of the breastplate's form after the pattern, square, doubled, and strong (the breastplate made foursquare and double: 'It was foursquare; they made the breastplate double: a span was the length thereof, and a span the breadth thereof, being doubled'; the form, 'foursquare,' that the breastplate was square, equal in length and breadth, a span each way, as commanded; the doubling, 'they made the breastplate double... being doubled,' that it was folded double, the stronger to bear the twelve precious stones and to endure; the meaning some draw, that the foursquare form may signify stability and perfection, and the doubling strength, the breastplate firm to bear the names of the tribes on the high priest's heart; the type before given, that the breastplate, square and strong, bearing the tribes, shadows Christ, our High Priest, who bears his people firmly and surely on his heart; that the making answered the command exactly). Note: the source for this verse carried a mislabeled cross-reference note on the candlestick, not pertaining to the breastplate; the synthesis follows the verse's actual subject.

themes: It was foursquare; they made the breastplate double
a span was the length thereof, and a span the breadth thereof, being doubled
the breastplate made foursquare and folded double, a span each way, strong to bear the stones
some seeing the foursquare form signify stability and the doubling strength; the breastplate shadowing Christ who bears his people firmly on his heart

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: they set in it four rows of stones, all these precious stones being particularly explained in the notes on Exodus 28:17; the witnesses note the setting of the four rows of stones in the breastplate, the first being a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle — the executing of the twelve precious stones, each for a tribe, after the pattern (the stones set in rows as commanded: 'they set in it four rows of stones: the first row was a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle'; the rows, 'four rows of stones,' twelve precious stones in all, three in each of four rows, as commanded; the first row named, 'a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle,' the several precious stones each set in its place; that each stone was engraved with a tribe's name, so that the high priest bore all the tribes, each as a several jewel, on his heart; the great type before given, that the twelve precious stones, each a tribe, shadow the saints, who are God's jewels, each precious and distinct, all borne on the heart of their High Priest; that God's people are precious stones in his sight, set in order and dear to him; that the making answered the command exactly).

debates.question: What do the twelve precious stones, each engraved with a tribe's name, shadow?

sides.position: The saints, who are God's jewels (Malachi 3:17) — each precious and distinct, a several jewel borne on the heart of their High Priest; God's people precious stones in his sight, set in order and dear to him

themes: And they set in it four rows of stones: the first row was a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle
this was the first row
the four rows of twelve precious stones set in the breastplate, each engraved with a tribe's name
the stones shadowing the saints, God's jewels, each precious and distinct, all borne on the heart of their High Priest

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the habergeon or hauberk was a small coat of mail, like a half shirt, made of small iron rings curiously united, which covered the neck and breast and resisted the stroke of a sword; the witnesses note the making of the hole in the midst of the robe, like the hole of a habergeon, with a band round about that it should not rend — the strong-bound opening of the robe, made firm so as not to tear (the robe's neck-hole bound that it should not rend: 'there was an hole in the midst of the robe, as the hole of an habergeon, with a band round about the hole, that it should not rend'; the opening, 'an hole in the midst of the robe,' the opening for the head, through which the robe was put on; the likeness, 'as the hole of an habergeon,' like the neck-opening of a small coat of mail, strong and firm, made as commanded; the binding, 'with a band round about the hole, that it should not rend,' that the edge was bound and woven, that the robe might not tear; the meaning some draw, that the robe, strongly bound that it should not rend, may shadow the seamless and unrending righteousness of Christ, whole and entire, never to be torn; the contrast some mark, that the high priest's robe was not to be rent, a rending of garments being a sign of grief, yet Christ's seamless robe was not rent at his death; that the making answered the command exactly).

themes: And there was an hole in the midst of the robe, as the hole of an habergeon, with a band round about the hole
that it should not rend
the neck-hole of the robe bound about, strong and firm, that it should not rend
some seeing it shadow the seamless and unrending righteousness of Christ, his seamless robe not rent at his death

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the pomegranates were of blue, and purple, and scarlet, twisted together, as before directed (Exodus 28:33); the witnesses note the making of pomegranates of blue, purple, scarlet, and twined linen upon the hems of the robe — the executing of the fruit-figures on the robe's skirts after the pattern (the pomegranates made on the hem: 'they made upon the hems of the robe pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and twined linen'; the pomegranates, 'pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and twined linen,' woven figures of the fruit, of the rich colours twisted together, set round the skirts of the robe, as commanded; the meaning some draw, that the pomegranate, full of seeds, signifies fruitfulness, and that these fruit-figures on the hem of the high priest's robe shadow the fruit of good works and graces that should adorn the walk of God's priests; that, set with the bells, the pomegranates, fruit, and the bells, sound, together signify fruit and sound, the life and the doctrine, the practice and the profession; that the making answered the command; that the priest's walk should be adorned with fruit).

themes: And they made upon the hems of the robe pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet
and twined linen
the pomegranates of the rich colours made on the hems of the robe, woven figures of the fruit
some seeing the pomegranate signify fruitfulness, the fruit-figures shadowing the good works adorning the priest's walk, fruit and sound together

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the bells of pure gold were set between the pomegranates, on the object of which see the note on Exodus 28:35, that their sound might be heard when the high priest went in and out; the witnesses note the making of bells of pure gold, set between the pomegranates round about the hem of the robe — the executing of the golden bells, whose sound went before the high priest, after the pattern (the bells set between the fruit: 'they made bells of pure gold, and put the bells between the pomegranates upon the hem of the robe, round about'; the bells, 'bells of pure gold,' golden bells, set between the pomegranates, alternating with the fruit round the hem, as commanded; the purpose, that their sound might be heard when the high priest went in to the holy place and came out, that he die not; the meaning some draw, that the bells, sound, and the pomegranates, fruit, together signify the sound of the gospel and the fruit of good works, the profession and the practice, the doctrine and the life, that should go together in God's ministers; that the sound of the bells, the testimony, was to be heard, and the fruit seen; that profession without fruit, or fruit without profession, is defective, but both together adorn the priest; that the making answered the command).

debates.question: What do the golden bells and the pomegranates on the hem of the robe signify?

sides.position: The bells (sound) and the pomegranates (fruit) together signify the sound of the gospel and the fruit of good works — the profession and the practice, the doctrine and the life — which should go together in God's ministers; profession without fruit, or fruit without profession, is defective, but both together adorn the priest

themes: they made bells of pure gold, and put the bells between the pomegranates upon the hem of the robe
round about between the pomegranates
the golden bells set between the pomegranates round the hem, their sound heard as the high priest went in and out
the bells (sound) and pomegranates (fruit) signifying profession and practice, doctrine and life, which should go together in God's ministers

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'they made coats,' rather tunics or shirts, of fine linen, for Aaron and his sons, as before directed (Exodus 28:40); the witnesses note the making of the coats of fine linen, of woven work, for Aaron and his sons — the executing of the white linen tunics after the pattern, the inner garments of the priests (the linen coats made: 'they made coats of fine linen of woven work for Aaron, and for his sons'; the coats, 'coats of fine linen of woven work,' the tunics or shirts of fine white linen, woven, the inner garments worn by both the high priest and his sons; that these were for Aaron, and for his sons, all the priests being clothed in linen; the meaning some draw, that the white fine linen signifies purity and righteousness, the righteousness of saints, the priests clothed in white as a figure of the holiness needful to serve God; that all who minister to God must be clothed in the white linen of righteousness; that the making answered the command; that the priests, high and common, were alike clothed in linen, all needing the same righteousness).

themes: And they made coats of fine linen of woven work for Aaron
and for his sons
the coats of fine white linen made for Aaron and his sons, the inner garments of the priests
some seeing the white linen signify purity and righteousness, the holiness needful to serve God, all clothed in the white linen of righteousness

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the mitre, or rather turban, was for Aaron, the goodly bonnets, or caps, for his sons, and the linen breeches, or drawers, for both Aaron and his sons; the witnesses note the making of the mitre of fine linen for Aaron, the bonnets for his sons, and the linen breeches for all — the executing of the head-tires and the breeches after the pattern, the rest of the linen garments (the mitre, bonnets, and breeches made: 'a mitre of fine linen, and goodly bonnets of fine linen, and linen breeches of fine twined linen'; the mitre, 'a mitre of fine linen,' a turban, for Aaron the high priest; the bonnets, 'goodly bonnets,' or caps, for his sons, the common priests; the breeches, 'linen breeches of fine twined linen,' or drawers, made both for Aaron and for his sons, to cover their nakedness in the service; the meaning some draw, that the linen breeches, covering nakedness, signify the need of covering for our shame in approaching God, and the modesty and purity required in his service; that all, from the head to the loins, was clothed in white linen, the figure of righteousness and purity; that the making answered the command).

themes: And a mitre of fine linen, and goodly bonnets of fine linen
and linen breeches of fine twined linen
the mitre for Aaron, the bonnets for his sons, and the linen breeches for all, made of fine linen
some seeing the breeches signify the covering of our shame before God; all clothed in white from head to loins, a figure of righteousness

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the girdle was for Aaron, described much more fully here than in the instructions, where it is called simply a girdle of needlework (Exodus 28:39); the witnesses note the making of the girdle of fine twined linen and the three colours, of needlework, as the LORD commanded Moses — the executing of the girdle, the last of the priestly garments, after the pattern (the girdle made, and all as commanded: 'a girdle of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, of needlework; as the LORD commanded Moses'; the girdle, 'a girdle of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, of needlework,' the sash of fine linen and the rich colours, wrought with the needle, for Aaron; the meaning some draw, that the girdle, which binds and girds, signifies the readiness, strength, and diligence needful for the priestly service, the loins girt for the work; the refrain, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' that this, like all the rest, was made exactly as commanded; that the girdle, the last garment, completes the priestly attire, all wrought after the pattern; that the making of every garment answered the command; that God's servants are to be girt and ready for his service).

themes: And a girdle of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, of needlework
as the LORD commanded Moses
the girdle made of fine linen and the rich colours, the last of the priestly garments, as the LORD commanded
some seeing the girdle signify the readiness, strength, and diligence needful for the priestly service, the loins girt for the work

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the holy crown of pure gold; on Asiatic and Egyptian monuments the priests are represented wearing crowns or tiaras, and the priests among the ancient Greeks were styled crown-bearers; Charles Ellicott: the plate of the holy crown, see the note on Exodus 29:6; the witnesses note the making of the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, engraved like a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD — the executing of the golden plate of holiness, the crown of the high priest, after the pattern (the crown of holiness made: 'they made the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, and wrote upon it a writing, like to the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD'; the plate, 'the plate of the holy crown of pure gold,' the golden plate fastened on the front of the mitre, called the holy crown, as commanded; the writing, 'like to the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD,' that these words were graven on it, as a seal is graven, marking the high priest as wholly consecrated to God; the great type before given, that the golden crown of holiness, worn on the forehead, shadows Christ, our High Priest, who is holiness itself, Holiness to the LORD, and who, bearing the iniquity of the holy things, makes the worship and the worshippers accepted; that the holiness of Christ, the crown of his priesthood, covers the defects of our holy things, that they may be accepted; that the making answered the command).

debates.question: What does the golden crown engraved 'HOLINESS TO THE LORD' shadow?

sides.position: Christ, our High Priest, who is holiness itself ('Holiness to the LORD') and who, bearing the iniquity of the holy things (Exodus 28:38), makes the worship and the worshippers accepted; his holiness, the crown of his priesthood, covering the defects of our holy things that they may be accepted

themes: they made the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, and wrote upon it a writing, like to the engravings of a signet
HOLINESS TO THE LORD
the golden plate of the holy crown made and engraved 'HOLINESS TO THE LORD,' marking the high priest wholly consecrated
a type of Christ who is holiness itself and, bearing the iniquity of the holy things, makes the worship and worshippers accepted

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the builders made very good despatch, it being not much more than five months from the beginning to the finishing; many hands were employed, all unanimous, the workmen taught of God, and the people hearty and zealous; and they punctually observed their orders, not varying in the least; the witnesses note the whole work of the tabernacle finished, the children of Israel doing all according to all the LORD commanded Moses — the completing of the whole work in exact obedience (all finished, and all done as commanded: 'Thus was all the work of the tabernacle... finished: and the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses'; the despatch, that the builders made very good despatch, finishing the whole in five months, though it was fine and curious work; the reasons Henry gives, that many hands were employed, all unanimous, the workmen taught of God, and the people hearty and zealous, so that the thing was done suddenly; the exactness, 'according to all that the LORD commanded Moses... so did they,' that they punctually observed their orders and did not in the least vary; the great rule the commentators draw, that God's work must be done according to his own will, his institutions neither needing nor admitting men's inventions, for God is pleased with willing worship but not with will-worship; that the work of God is to be done exactly as he commands).

debates.question: What does the tabernacle being made exactly 'according to all that the LORD commanded' teach about worship?

sides.position: That God's work must be done in everything according to his own will — his institutions neither needing nor admitting men's inventions to make them more beautiful or effectual; God is pleased with willing worship, but not with will-worship ('Add thou not unto his words')

themes: Thus was all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of the congregation finished
and the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses, so did they
all the work finished with great despatch and exact obedience, done as the LORD commanded
God's work must be done according to his own will, admitting no men's inventions; willing worship, not will-worship

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: they brought all their work to Moses, and submitted it to his inspection; he knew what he had ordered them to make, and now the particulars were called over, that Moses might see they had made all, omitting nothing, and all according to the instructions; the witnesses note the people bringing the tabernacle, the tent, and all its furniture, taches, boards, bars, pillars, and sockets to Moses — the submitting of all the finished work to Moses for inspection (they bring all and submit it to be examined: 'they brought the tabernacle unto Moses, the tent, and all his furniture, his taches, his boards, his bars, and his pillars, and his sockets'; the bringing, 'they brought the tabernacle unto Moses... and all his furniture,' that the several parts, before they were set up, were brought and submitted to his inspection; the listing, 'his taches, his boards, his bars, and his pillars, and his sockets,' that the particulars were called over and all produced, that Moses might see they had made all, omitting nothing, and according to the instructions; the humility the commentators mark, that, though the workmen were taught of God and knew the work, they had also so much humility as to be willing to have it examined and compared with the model, paying deference to Moses, who was set over them in the Lord; that the spirit of the prophets is subject to the prophets; that the gifted are to submit their work to lawful authority).

themes: they brought the tabernacle unto Moses, the tent, and all his furniture
his taches, his boards, his bars, and his pillars, and his sockets
the people bringing all the finished work to Moses and submitting it to his inspection
the humility that, though taught of God, they had their work examined and compared with the model; the spirit of the prophets subject to the prophets

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the vail of the covering, that is, the vail which separated between the holy place and the holy of holies, covering the latter from the sight of man; the witnesses note the coverings of rams' skins dyed red and of badgers' skins, and the veil of the covering, brought to Moses among the finished work — the presenting of the coverings and the veil for inspection (these too brought and shown: 'the covering of rams' skins dyed red, and the covering of badgers' skins, and the vail of the covering'; the coverings, 'the covering of rams' skins dyed red, and... of badgers' skins,' the two outer coverings, brought with the rest; the veil, 'the vail of the covering,' that is, the vail which separated between the holy place and the holy of holies, covering the latter from the sight of man; that all these parts, like the rest, were produced before Moses, that he might see all was made and made aright; the types before given, that the rams' and badgers' skins shadow Christ's atoning sufferings and lowly appearance, and the veil his flesh, dividing yet, under the law, the way to the holiest; that every part was brought and submitted, none kept back).

themes: And the covering of rams' skins dyed red, and the covering of badgers' skins
and the vail of the covering
the two outer coverings and the veil brought to Moses among the finished work
the rams' and badgers' skins shadowing Christ's sufferings and lowly appearance, the veil his flesh, all the parts submitted

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the pure candlestick, the lamps to be set in order, that is, to be trimmed and fresh oiled every day, for the purpose of being lighted in the evening; the witnesses note the pure candlestick, with its lamps to be set in order, all its vessels, and the oil for light, brought to Moses — the presenting of the candlestick and its furniture for inspection (the candlestick and its light brought: 'the pure candlestick, with the lamps thereof... and all the vessels thereof, and the oil for light'; the candlestick, 'the pure candlestick, with the lamps thereof,' the golden candlestick of the holy place, with its seven lamps; the lamps, 'the lamps to be set in order,' that is, to be trimmed and fresh oiled every day, for being lighted in the evening; the vessels and oil, 'all the vessels thereof, and the oil for light,' the snuffers and dishes, and the pure oil for the light; the type before given, that the candlestick, giving light in the holy place, shadows Christ the light of the world, and the church bearing his light, fed by the oil of the Spirit and tended daily; that the light must be daily trimmed and supplied, the believer's light kept burning by daily grace; that all was brought and shown to Moses).

themes: The pure candlestick, with the lamps thereof, even with the lamps to be set in order, and all the vessels thereof
and the oil for light
the pure candlestick with its lamps, vessels, and oil brought to Moses, the lamps trimmed and oiled daily
the candlestick shadowing Christ the light and the church bearing his light, fed by the oil of the Spirit, tended by daily grace

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the sweet incense, and the hanging for the tabernacle door, all brought among the finished work for Moses to see; the witnesses note the golden altar of incense, the anointing oil, the sweet incense, and the hanging for the door brought to Moses — the presenting of the incense-altar, oil, incense, and door for inspection (these holy things brought and shown: 'the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the sweet incense, and the hanging for the tabernacle door'; the golden altar, 'the golden altar,' the altar of incense, of the holy place; the oil and incense, 'the anointing oil, and the sweet incense,' the holy oil and the sweet incense, each of its appointed composition; the door, 'the hanging for the tabernacle door,' the rich screen at the entrance; the types before given, that the golden altar and the sweet incense shadow the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession, a sweet savour before God, the anointing oil the Spirit, and the door hanging Christ the door, by whom we enter; that all these holy things were brought and produced before Moses, none wanting; that the whole was made according to the command).

themes: And the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the sweet incense
and the hanging for the tabernacle door
the golden altar of incense, the anointing oil, the sweet incense, and the door hanging brought to Moses
the altar and incense shadowing Christ's intercession, the oil the Spirit, the door Christ the door, all produced, none wanting

schools.summary: Jamieson, Fausset and Brown: the tabernacle, from its name and appearance, was a tent, yet from the description, the boards that formed its walls, the bars that strengthened them, and the middle bar that held all, gave it strength and stability; the witnesses note the brazen altar with its grate of brass, staves, and vessels, and the laver and its foot, brought to Moses — the presenting of the vessels of the court for inspection (the altar and laver brought and shown: 'the brasen altar, and his grate of brass, his staves, and all his vessels, the laver and his foot'; the brazen altar, 'the brasen altar, and his grate of brass, his staves, and all his vessels,' the altar of burnt offering, with its grate, staves for carriage, and pans, shovels, and basins; the laver, 'the laver and his foot,' the brazen laver, with its base, where the priests washed; the types before given, that the brazen altar shadows the cross of Christ, where sacrifice is made, and the laver the washing of regeneration and the word, the way to God being through sacrifice and cleansing; that these vessels of the court, the first met on entering, were brought and produced before Moses; that all was made according to the command; that, though the tabernacle was but a tent, it was given strength and stability by its frame).

themes: The brasen altar, and his grate of brass, his staves, and all his vessels
the laver and his foot
the brazen altar with its grate, staves, and vessels, and the laver, brought to Moses
the brazen altar shadowing the cross of Christ, the laver the washing of regeneration, the way to God through sacrifice and cleansing

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the cloths of service to do service in the holy place, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments, to minister in the priest's office, brought among the finished work, all made according to all the LORD commanded; the witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron and his sons brought to Moses, to minister in the priest's office — the presenting of the priestly vestments, the last of the finished work, for inspection (the garments too brought and shown: 'the cloths of service... and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments, to minister in the priest's office'; the cloths of service, 'the cloths of service to do service in the holy place,' the cloths used in the ministry; the holy garments, 'the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments,' all the priestly vestments, before described, to minister in the priest's office; that these, with all the rest, were brought and submitted to Moses, all made according to all the LORD commanded; the type before given, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ and his people are clothed for God's service; that all the work, garments and vessels alike, was now complete and produced; that nothing was wanting, all made as commanded).

themes: The cloths of service to do service in the holy place, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments
to minister in the priest's office
the cloths of service and the holy garments of the priests brought to Moses, the last of the finished work
the holy garments shadowing the righteousness with which Christ and his people are clothed; all complete, nothing wanting, all as commanded

schools.summary: Gill: Moses made a survey of it, examined every particular with great care, and behold, they had done it as the LORD commanded, both as to matter and manner in every point, and Moses blessed them, commending them and imploring the divine blessing; the Targum's form, may the Shechinah of the LORD dwell in the works of your hands; Adam Clarke: Moses, the general superintendent, looked upon all the work; they had done it exactly according to the pattern; and Moses blessed them, very probably praying that they might be prospered and brought at last to the inheritance that fadeth not away; the witnesses note Moses looking on all the work, finding it done exactly as the LORD commanded, and blessing the people — the inspection and blessing, the close of the work like the close of creation (Moses surveying all, finding it right, and blessing: 'Moses did look upon all the work, and, behold, they had done it as the LORD had commanded... and Moses blessed them'; the survey, that he examined every particular with great care and accuracy; the verdict, that all was done both as to matter and manner just as ordered, for they had been filled by the Spirit of God; the blessing, that he commended them and implored the divine blessing on the givers and the workmen; the great type Clarke draws, that the tabernacle, on which God's glory rested, typified the human nature of the Lord Jesus, in whom the Word was made flesh and dwelt, tabernacled, among us, full of grace and truth, the fulness of the Godhead dwelling in him; that, as Moses surveyed all and blessed, so the close of God's work is his approval and blessing, like the close of creation).

debates.question: What does the tabernacle, on which God's glory rested, typify?

sides.position: The human nature of the Lord Jesus — in whom 'the Word was made flesh and dwelt (tabernacled) among us, full of grace and truth' (John 1:14), the fulness of the Godhead dwelling bodily in him; as the glory rested on the tabernacle, so the divine glory rested on Christ

themes: Moses did look upon all the work, and, behold, they had done it as the LORD had commanded, even so had they done it
and Moses blessed them
Moses surveying all the work, finding it done as the LORD commanded, and blessing the givers and the workmen
the tabernacle typifying the human nature of Christ, 'the Word made flesh and tabernacled among us'; the close of the work like the close of creation
